full_notes:
Bricks Beads and Bones is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 1 Bricks, Beads and Bones The Harappan Civilisation THEME ONE The Harappan seal (Fig.1.1) is possibly the most distinctive artefact of the Harappan or Indus valley civilisation.

The text explains that made of a stone called steatite, seals like this one often contain animal motifs and signs from a script that remains undeciphered.

The text explains that yet we know a great deal about the lives of the people who lived in the region from what they left behind – their houses, pots, ornaments, tools and seals – in other words, from archaeological evidence.

The text explains that let us see what we know about the Harappan civilisation, and how we know about it.

The text explains that we will explore how archaeological material is interpreted and how interpretations sometimes change.

The text explains that of course, there are some aspects of the civilisation that are as yet unknown and may even remain so.

The text explains that terminologies, Places and Time “The Harappan Civilisation” is also known as the Indus Valley Civilisation.

The text explains that the term Harappa is derived from the place where the civilization was identified.

The text explains that the total time span of the civilisation ranges from 6000 BCE to 1300 BCE.

The text explains that the early phase, termed as Early Harappan (6000 BCE-2600 BCE) is a formative phase of the civilisation.

The text explains that the urban phase of the civilisation, termed as Mature Harappan (2600 BCE-1900 BCE) is the most prosperous phase.

The text explains that the decline of the civilisation starts around 1900 BCE.

The text explains that this decadent phase (1900 BCE-1300 BCE) is termed as Late Harappan.

The text explains that the distinctive Harappan pottery, bricks (baked and unbaked) seals, weights, beads, copper and bronze articles, belonging to the Harappan civilisation have been found in the region, as apart as Afghanistan, Baluchistan, Sind and Punjab provinces of Pakistan and the Indian States of Jammu and Kashmir, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat and Maharashtra (Map 1).

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 2 Map 1 Some important Mature Harappan sites Sketch map not to scale Manda Harappa Banawali Rakhigarhi Mitathal Ganweriwala Kot Diji Chanhudaro Amri Balakot Mohenjodaro Sutkagendor Dholavira Lothal Nageshwar Rangpur Kalibangan Jhelum Chenab Ravi Indus Yamuna Ganga Chambal Sabarmati Arabian Sea Mahi Narmada Sutlej 1.

The text explains that beginnings There were several archaeological cultures in the region prior to the Mature Harappan.

The text explains that the present year is 2015 according to this dating system. c. stands for the Latin word circa and means “approximate.”

The text explains that the field had two sets of furrows at right angles to each other, suggesting that two different crops were grown together.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 4 Ü Do you think these tools could have been used for harvesting?

The text explains that 1.6 Saddle quern Source 1 canals silted up long ago.

The text explains that how artefacts are identified Processing of food required grinding equipment as well as vessels for mixing, blending and cooking.

The text explains that as a rule, they were roughly made of hard, gritty, igneous rock or sandstone and mostly show signs of hard usage.

The text explains that as their bases are usually convex, they must have been set in the earth or in mud to prevent their rocking.

The text explains that mohanjodaro A Planned Urban Centre Perhaps the most unique feature of the Harappan civilisation was the development of urban centres.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 6 The plight of Harappa Although Harappa was the first site to be discovered, it was badly destroyed by brick robbers.

The text explains that as early as 1875, Alexander Cunningham, the first Director-General of the A r c h a e o l o g i c a l S u r v e y o f India (ASI), often called the father of Indian archaeology, n o t e d t h a t t h e a m o u n t o f b r i c k t a k e n f r o m t h e a n c i e n t s i t e w a s e n o u g h t o l a y b r i c k s f o r “ a b o u t 1 0 0 m i l e s ” o f t h e r a i l w a y l i n e b e t w e e n L a h o r e a n d Multan.

The text explains that thus, many of the ancient structures at the site were damaged.

The text explains that it has been calculated that if one labourer moved roughly a cubic metre of earth daily, just to put the foundations in place it would have required four million person-days, in other words, mobilising labour on a very large scale.

The text explains that 3.1 Laying out drains One of the most distinctive features of Harappan cities was the carefully planned drainage system.

The text explains that if you look at the plan of the Lower Town you will notice that roads and streets were laid out along an approximate “grid” pattern, intersecting at right angles.

The text explains that besides, the main entrance does not give a direct view of the interior or the courtyard.

The text explains that the most ancient system yet discovered About the drains, Mackay noted: “It is certainly the most complete ancient system as yet discovered.” Every house was connected to the street drains.

The text explains that drainage systems were not unique to the larger cities, but were found in smaller settlements as well.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 8 3.3 The Citadel It is on the Citadel that we find evidence of structures that were probably used for special public purposes.

The text explains that these include the warehouse – a massive structure of which the lower brick portions remain, while the upper portions, probably of wood, decayed long ago – and the Great Bath.

The text explains that the uniqueness of the structure, as well as the context in which it was found (the Citadel, with several distinctive buildings), has led scholars to suggest that it was meant for some kind of a special ritual bath.

The text explains that which of the architectural features of Mohenjodaro indicate planning? Ü Are there other structures on the Citadel apart from the warehouse and the Great Bath?

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 10 use, such as spindle whorls made of rare materials such as faience.

The text explains that if for some reason the original owners do not retrieve them, they remain where they are left till some archaeologist finds them.

The text explains that grinding, polishing and drilling completed the process.

The text explains that should the stone artefacts illustrated in the chapter be considered as utilitarian objects or as luxuries?

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 12 Sometimes, larger waste pieces were used up to make smaller objects, but minuscule bits were usually left in the work area.

The text explains that other such sites were Shortughai, in far-off Afghanistan, near the best source of lapis lazuli, a blue stone that was apparently very highly valued, and Lothal which was near sources of carnelian (from Bharuch in Gujarat), steatite (from south Rajasthan and north Gujarat) and metal (from Rajasthan).

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 14 Mesopotamian sites also contains traces of nickel.

The text explains that it is apparent that the script was written from right to left as some seals show a wider spacing on the right and cramping on the left, as if the engraver began working from the right and then ran out of space.

The text explains that 7.3 Weights Exchanges were regulated by a precise system of weights, usually made of a stone called chert and generally cubical (Fig.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 16 lower denominations of weights were binary (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32), while the higher denominations followed the decimal system (160, 200, 320, 640).

The text explains that ancient Authority There are indications of complex decisions being taken and implemented in Harappan society.

The text explains that we have also seen that settlements were strategically set up in specific locations for various reasons.

The text explains that this is because archaeologists were familiar with Mesopotamian history and its “priest-kings” and have found parallels in the Indus region.

The text explains that some archaeologists are of the opinion that Harappan society had no rulers, and that everybody enjoyed equal status.

The text explains that discuss how archaeologists reconstruct the past.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History I, Chapter 1: Bricks Beads and Bones

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Bricks Beads and Bones, which statement is supported by the source text about BRICKS, BEADS AND BONES?
options: These cultures were associated with distinctive pottery, evidence of agriculture and pastoralism, and some crafts.
Settlements were generally small, and there were virtually no large buildings.
The recent archaeological evidences also indicate the emergence of Harappan culture from early farming communities dated around 7000 BCE.
The urban phase (Mature Harappan) was a consequent result of the gradual transformation and internal development of the civilisation during the early Harappan phase.
